Lesson 1 of 3

Neural network fundamentals

Perceptrons, activation functions, backpropagation, and optimization form the building blocks of all deep learning.

30-40 min AI/ML flow Deep learning 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Understanding how gradients flow through layers is essential for debugging training failures and designing effective architectures.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Architecture basics Each neuron computes a weighted sum followed by a non-linear activation2. Training mechanics Backpropagation applies the chain rule layer by layer to compute gradients3. Practical considerations Xavier/He initialization prevents early gradient collapse

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Understanding how gradients flow through layers is essential for debugging training failures and designing effective architectures. 2. Architecture basics Each neuron computes a weighted sum followed by a non-linear activation3. Training mechanics Backpropagation applies the chain rule layer by layer to compute gradients4. Practical considerations Xavier/He initialization prevents early gradient collapse
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Understanding how gradients flow through layers is essential for debugging training failures and designing effective architectures.

What to say first

  • Can trace a forward and backward pass through a simple network on paper.
  • Understands why ReLU helps with vanishing gradients compared to sigmoid.
  • Knows when to adjust learning rate, batch size, or initialization.
2

Study section

Architecture basics

Neurons, layers, activation functions (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh), and the universal approximation theorem explain why networks can model complex functions.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Each neuron computes a weighted sum followed by a non-linear activation
  • Depth allows hierarchical feature learning; width allows richer representations per layer
  • ReLU dominates hidden layers due to efficient gradients; softmax serves classification outputs

Code example

Define and train a neural network with PyTorch

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# 2-layer neural network for classification
class SimpleNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, input_dim, hidden_dim, num_classes):
        super().__init__()
        self.layers = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(input_dim, hidden_dim),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden_dim, num_classes),
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.layers(x)

model = SimpleNet(input_dim=10, hidden_dim=64, num_classes=3)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

# Training loop (one batch)
X = torch.randn(32, 10)           # batch of 32 samples
y = torch.randint(0, 3, (32,))    # random labels

logits = model(X)
loss = loss_fn(logits, y)
loss.backward()                    # compute gradients
optimizer.step()                   # update weights
print(f"Loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
3

Study section

Training mechanics

Forward pass, loss computation, backward pass (backpropagation), and parameter updates via optimizers (SGD, Adam) form the training loop.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Backpropagation applies the chain rule layer by layer to compute gradients
  • Adam adapts learning rates per parameter using momentum and variance estimates
  • Batch normalization and learning rate schedules stabilize training
4

Study section

Practical considerations

GPU utilization, batch size effects, initialization strategies, and debugging vanishing/exploding gradients.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Xavier/He initialization prevents early gradient collapse
  • Larger batch sizes improve throughput but may hurt generalization
  • Gradient clipping prevents exploding gradients in deep or recurrent networks

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Can trace a forward and backward pass through a simple network on paper.
  • Understands why ReLU helps with vanishing gradients compared to sigmoid.
  • Knows when to adjust learning rate, batch size, or initialization.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Training without monitoring loss curves and validation metrics.
  • Using overly complex architectures before proving a simpler one fails.
  • Ignoring data preprocessing that causes numerical instability.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Walk through backpropagation for a two-layer network.
  • Why might training loss decrease while validation loss increases?
  • Compare SGD with momentum to Adam. When would you prefer each?

Go beyond the overview with practical examples

1 code example 1 design drill

What to master

Study this in your own words

  • Can trace a forward and backward pass through a simple network on paper.
  • Understands why ReLU helps with vanishing gradients compared to sigmoid.
  • Knows when to adjust learning rate, batch size, or initialization.

Production lens

Failure modes to call out

  • Training without monitoring loss curves and validation metrics.
  • Using overly complex architectures before proving a simpler one fails.
  • Ignoring data preprocessing that causes numerical instability.

Practice aloud

Questions worth rehearsing

  • Walk through backpropagation for a two-layer network.
  • Why might training loss decrease while validation loss increases?
  • Compare SGD with momentum to Adam. When would you prefer each?

Code example

Build a neural network from scratch

intermediate coding

Implement a two-layer neural network in NumPy with forward pass, ReLU activation, cross-entropy loss, and backpropagation. Train it on XOR.

What to notice

  • Forward: z1 = X @ W1 + b1, a1 = ReLU(z1), z2 = a1 @ W2 + b2, output = sigmoid(z2)
  • Backward: start from output error and chain rule back through each layer
  • ReLU derivative is 1 where input > 0, else 0

Expected outcome

Network should learn XOR after ~5000 iterations with correct outputs near 0 or 1

Starter code 
import numpy as np

class TwoLayerNet:
    def __init__(self, input_dim, hidden_dim, output_dim):
        self.W1 = np.random.randn(input_dim, hidden_dim) * 0.01
        self.b1 = np.zeros(hidden_dim)
        self.W2 = np.random.randn(hidden_dim, output_dim) * 0.01
        self.b2 = np.zeros(output_dim)

    def forward(self, X):
        # TODO: Implement forward pass with ReLU
        pass

    def backward(self, X, y, learning_rate=0.1):
        # TODO: Implement backpropagation
        pass

# Train on XOR
X = np.array([[0,0],[0,1],[1,0],[1,1]])
y = np.array([[0],[1],[1],[0]])
net = TwoLayerNet(2, 8, 1)
Reference solution 
import numpy as np

class TwoLayerNet:
    def __init__(self, input_dim, hidden_dim, output_dim):
        self.W1 = np.random.randn(input_dim, hidden_dim) * 0.01
        self.b1 = np.zeros(hidden_dim)
        self.W2 = np.random.randn(hidden_dim, output_dim) * 0.01
        self.b2 = np.zeros(output_dim)

    def forward(self, X):
        self.z1 = X @ self.W1 + self.b1
        self.a1 = np.maximum(0, self.z1)  # ReLU
        self.z2 = self.a1 @ self.W2 + self.b2
        self.output = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-self.z2))  # Sigmoid
        return self.output

    def backward(self, X, y, learning_rate=0.1):
        m = X.shape[0]
        dz2 = self.output - y
        dW2 = self.a1.T @ dz2 / m
        db2 = dz2.mean(axis=0)
        da1 = dz2 @ self.W2.T
        dz1 = da1 * (self.z1 > 0)
        dW1 = X.T @ dz1 / m
        db1 = dz1.mean(axis=0)
        self.W2 -= learning_rate * dW2
        self.b2 -= learning_rate * db2
        self.W1 -= learning_rate * dW1
        self.b1 -= learning_rate * db1

Design drill

Demonstrate vanishing gradients

beginner

Design an experiment that shows vanishing gradients with sigmoid activations in a deep network vs ReLU. Explain what you would measure and expect to observe.

Use these prompts to deepen the study pass

  • What happens to the gradient of sigmoid when inputs are very large or very small?
  • How does this compound across 10+ layers during backpropagation?
  • Why does ReLU not suffer from this problem (and what is its own failure mode)?
  • How would you monitor gradient magnitudes during training?

Interactive topic lab

Neural network training lab

Interactive diagram

Neural-network training loop

Rendering diagram…

Treat training as a full system: data, optimization, validation, and thresholding all determine production behavior.

Worked example

Use layered representations when raw pixels are the bottleneck

A manufacturing line needs to detect product defects from camera images where handcrafted thresholds fail across lighting and product variants.

Recommended choice

Train a convolutional neural network with augmentation and a validation pipeline that tracks recall on rare defect classes.

Why it fits

  • CNN layers learn edges, textures, and part-level patterns that manual thresholds do not capture.
  • Data augmentation makes the model robust to lighting, rotation, and camera variation.
  • Per-class recall prevents the model from ignoring rare but expensive defects.

If you chose differently

Feature-engineering alone may work for one camera setup, but it usually breaks when the defect pattern or imaging conditions shift.

Interview-ready takeaway

The strong explanation is not "deep learning is better", but "representation learning handles variability that brittle rules cannot."

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Explain the concept

Explain "Neural network fundamentals" as if teaching a senior engineer who is new to AI/ML. Cover the core intuition, when and why you would use it, and the key algorithms or techniques involved.

Goal

Demonstrate deep understanding by explaining the topic clearly, covering intuition, algorithms, and practical applications.

Guardrails

  • Walk through backpropagation for a two-layer network.
  • Architecture basics
  • Training mechanics
  • Can trace a forward and backward pass through a simple network on paper.

Suggested Structure

  • Core intuition
  • Key algorithms or techniques
  • When to apply
  • Common variants
